sentences.chinese: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。
sentences.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and more than ten others in crimson garb shaved their own heads and put on the cangue, following the king to Chang'an.
sentences.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen companions donned convict russet, shaved their own heads, and clamped on the iron collar to escort their king to Chang'an.

sentences.chinese: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。
sentences.literal: King Ao of Zhao's case was cleared; he was able to leave, was removed as king and made Marquis of Xuanping, and then advanced word recommending Shu and the ten men.
sentences.idiomatic: When Zhang Ao's name was cleared, he left prison, was stripped of his kingship and enfeoffed as Marquis of Xuanping, and then recommended Tian Shu and his nine companions to the throne.

sentences.chinese: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。
sentences.literal: The sovereign summoned them for audience and conversed with them; no minister of the Han court could surpass them.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor received them and spoke with each in turn; not one minister at court could match their poise.

sentences.chinese: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。
sentences.literal: The sovereign was pleased and appointed them all governors or chancellors to the feudal lords.
sentences.idiomatic: Pleased with them all, he named every man either a commandery governor or a feudal chancellor.

sentences.chinese: 叔為漢中守十餘年。
sentences.literal: Shu was governor of Hanzhong for more than ten years.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for well over a decade.

sentences.chinese: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」
sentences.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen had just taken the throne, he summoned Shu and asked, 'Do you know who are the most worthy elders in the realm?'
sentences.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Xiaowen's reign he called Tian Shu in and asked, 'Who in the empire commands respect as a true elder statesman?'

sentences.chinese: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」
sentences.literal: He replied, 'Your servant how could be sufficient to know that!'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, 'How could a man like me presume to judge such things?'

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, 'You are an elder; you ought to know.'
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, 'You yourself are such a man; you must have an opinion.'

sentences.chinese: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」
sentences.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, 'The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is such a man.'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, 'The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is the one I would name.'

sentences.chinese: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。
sentences.literal: At that time Meng Shu sat convicted because the barbarians had entered Yunzhong in great force and was dismissed.
sentences.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been stripped of office for letting a large Xiongnu raid penetrate Yunzhong.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, 'The late emperor established Meng Shu in Yunzhong for more than ten years; the barbarians once entered in force; Meng Shu could not hold firm; for no reason soldiers died in battle several hundred men—do worthies indeed kill people?'
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor objected: 'The late emperor kept Meng Shu at Yunzhong for years; the Xiongnu broke in once in strength, he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of men died without cause. Is that what you call the conduct of a humane elder?'

sentences.chinese: 長者固殺人乎？」
sentences.literal: 'Do men of virtue go about killing people?'
sentences.idiomatic: Do worthy men make a habit of sending men to their deaths?'

sentences.chinese: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！
sentences.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, 'When Guan Gao and the others plotted rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty to the third degree of kin; yet Meng Shu shaved his own head and put on the cangue to follow King Zhang, ready to die with him—how could he have known he would become governor of Yunzhong!'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and replied, 'When Guan Gao conspired treason, the court decreed that anyone in Zhao who stayed with King Zhang Ao would die with three degrees of kin—yet Meng Shu mutilated himself like a convict and followed his lord into captivity, prepared to die. He could not then have dreamed he would one day govern Yunzhong.'

sentences.chinese: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！
sentences.literal: Han and Chu faced each other; the soldiers were worn out, while the Xiongnu Modu had newly subdued the northern tribes and came as border raiders. Meng Shu knew the troops were exhausted and could not bear to order them out; the men vied to mount the walls and die against the foe like sons for a father—therefore several hundred died. How could Meng Shu have driven them!'
sentences.idiomatic: Han and Chu were locked in stalemate and the army was spent; Modu had just cowed the northern peoples and turned south to harry the frontier. Meng Shu saw how broken his men were and could not bring himself to drive them out; the soldiers themselves rushed to the walls and died like sons defending a father—hence the heavy losses. Meng Shu did not hound them to that fate.'

sentences.chinese: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」
sentences.literal: This is the reason Meng Shu is a worthy elder.'
sentences.idiomatic: That is precisely what makes Meng Shu a true elder.'

sentences.chinese: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」
sentences.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said, 'How worthy is Meng Shu!'
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, 'Meng Shu is a remarkable man!'

sentences.chinese: 復召以為雲中守。
sentences.literal: He again summoned him and made him governor of Yunzhong.
sentences.idiomatic: He recalled Meng Shu and restored him as governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.chinese: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。
sentences.literal: Several years later Shu lost office because of a legal offense.
sentences.idiomatic: Some years afterward Tian Shu lost his post over a legal infraction.

sentences.chinese: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。
sentences.literal: Prince Xiao of Liang had someone kill the Han councilor Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang, and he fully obtained the facts.
sentences.idiomatic: When Prince Xiao of Liang had the Han adviser Yuan Ang murdered, Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to probe the kingdom of Liang, and he extracted the whole truth.

sentences.chinese: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」
sentences.literal: Returning, he reported; the sovereign said, 'Did Liang have it?'
sentences.idiomatic: On his return the emperor asked him bluntly, 'Did Liang do it?'

sentences.chinese: 對曰：「有之。」
sentences.literal: He replied, 'There is.'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, 'Yes.'

sentences.chinese: 「事安在？」
sentences.literal: 'Where is the affair?'
sentences.idiomatic: 'What proof do you have?'

sentences.chinese: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。
sentences.literal: Shu said, 'Your Majesty had best not press the matter of Liang.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, 'Your Majesty should not pursue this question about Liang.'

sentences.chinese: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；
sentences.literal: Now if the Prince of Liang is not executed, this is casting aside the law of Han;
sentences.idiomatic: If the Prince of Liang goes unpunished, you abandon the statutes of Han;

sentences.chinese: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」
sentences.literal: if he is executed, the empress dowager will not find her food sweet nor her couch easy—this care falls on Your Majesty.'
sentences.idiomatic: if you execute him, the empress dowager will lose her appetite and her sleep—the burden of that grief will rest on you alone.'

sentences.chinese: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him chancellor of Lu.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor admired his tact and appointed him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.chinese: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。
sentences.literal: When the chancellor first took office, commoners who on their own spoke out that the king had taken their goods numbered more than a hundred.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon after he took office, more than a hundred people came forward on their own, accusing the king of seizing their property.

sentences.chinese: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？
sentences.literal: Shu took their ringleaders, two thousand men, and flogged them, angrily saying, 'Is the king not your lord? How dare you speak against your lord on your own!'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu had the ringleaders—some two thousand men—rounded up and flogged, shouting, 'The king is your sovereign; how dare you denounce him in open court!'

sentences.chinese: 何敢自言主！」
sentences.literal: 'How dare you on your own speak against the lord!'
sentences.idiomatic: Who gave you leave to accuse your master?'

sentences.chinese: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。
sentences.literal: The King of Lu heard, was greatly ashamed, opened the central treasury's cash, and had the chancellor compensate them.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Lu was mortified, opened the palace treasury, and told the chancellor to pay the claimants.

sentences.chinese: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」
sentences.literal: The chancellor said, 'Let the king himself send men to make restitution; if not, it is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good.'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu refused: 'Send your own stewards to make amends; otherwise it will look as though you committed the wrong and I bought cheap virtue.'

sentences.chinese: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。
sentences.literal: The King of Lu loved hunting; the chancellor often followed him into the park, and the king always had the chancellor rest and go to the lodge.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; Tian Shu always rode with him into the hunting park, and the king would dismiss him to the guesthouse to rest.

sentences.chinese: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」
sentences.literal: The chancellor often sat exposed outside the park and to the end did not rest, saying, 'Our king is exposed to wind and sun—why alone should I take shelter?'
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Shu instead sat in the open outside the preserve and refused to leave, saying, 'If my king endures the weather, how can I hide indoors?'

sentences.chinese: 王以故不大出遊。
sentences.literal: For this reason the king for the most part did not go out roaming.
sentences.idiomatic: Shamed into it, the king gave up most of his long hunting trips.

sentences.chinese: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」
sentences.literal: Several years later he died in office; Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for sacrifice; the younger son Ren would not accept, saying, 'By righteousness I do not harm the prior man's name.'
sentences.idiomatic: He died in office a few years later. Lu offered a hundred pounds of gold for his rites, but his youngest son Tian Ren declined, saying, 'Right feeling forbids me to trade on my father's reputation.'

sentences.chinese: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。
sentences.literal: Ren for strength and courage was a retainer of General Wei and repeatedly followed in striking the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Ren, bold and hardy, served as a household retainer to General Wei Qing and rode with him on many campaigns against the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。
sentences.literal: General Wei advanced word recommending Ren as gentleman of the palace; he reached two thousand shi and chief clerk to the chancellor, then lost office.
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing recommended him for gentleman of the palace; he rose to two thousand shi and chief clerk under the chancellor, then lost his post.

sentences.chinese: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。
sentences.literal: Later he was sent to censor the Three He region; returning, his memorial pleased the sovereign, and he was appointed metropolitan commandant.
sentences.idiomatic: Later he was sent as imperial censor to the Three He circuit; his memorial on returning pleased the emperor, and he was appointed metropolitan commandant of the capital region.

sentences.chinese: 月餘，遷司直。
sentences.literal: After more than a month he was promoted to imperial investigator.
sentences.idiomatic: A little over a month later he was promoted to imperial investigator.

sentences.chinese: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。
sentences.literal: Several years later, when Crown Prince Li raised troops, Ren's section closed the city gates so that the crown prince could flee; he sat convicted for letting the rebel go and his clan was exterminated.
sentences.idiomatic: Years later, when Heir Apparent Liu Ju rose in arms, Tian Ren, commanding his detachment, had the city gates shut in a manner that allowed the prince to slip away; he was convicted of releasing a traitor and his whole clan was put to death.

sentences.chinese: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。
sentences.literal: The appraisal says: With Xiang Yu's fierce temper, yet Ji Bu by courage displayed his name in Chu; personally he trod battle lines and tore enemy banners many times—he may be called a stalwart.
sentences.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: For all of Xiang Yu's towering rage, Ji Bu won fame in Chu through sheer nerve, time and again charging the line and snatching enemy standards—a true fighting man.

sentences.chinese: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？
sentences.literal: Coming to being trapped and humiliated as a slave, clinging to life without changing—why?
sentences.idiomatic: Yet when ruin drove him to the disgrace of slavery, he endured and did not break—why was that?

sentences.chinese: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。
sentences.literal: They trusted their own talents; receiving insult without shame, they wished to apply what still lay unused in them; therefore in the end they became famous generals of Han.
sentences.idiomatic: They trusted their own ability; they could bear shame because they still had more to give the world, and so they finished as celebrated generals of Han.

sentences.chinese: 賢者誠重其死。
sentences.literal: Worthy men indeed take their deaths seriously.
sentences.idiomatic: Men of true worth do not throw their lives away cheaply.

sentences.chinese: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。
sentences.literal: As for maids, concubines, and mean persons, stirred to indignation and killing themselves—they cannot be called brave; their scheming is utterly shallow.
sentences.idiomatic: Maids, concubines, and common folk who take offense and fall on their blades are not brave; their outlook is hopelessly mean and narrow.

sentences.chinese: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！
sentences.literal: Luan Bu wept for Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao; they went to death as if returning home—they truly knew their place; though the steadfast heroes of antiquity, what could surpass them!
sentences.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourned Peng Yue at the risk of his life; Tian Shu shackled himself to Zhang Ao and marched toward execution as calmly as a man going home. They knew exactly where they stood; the steadfast worthies of old could scarcely have outdone them.
